questions.answer: A. The program implements minimax search; alpha-beta pruning can reduce search
questions.to_find: Working principle of the game-tree search program and a mechanism to reduce search

understand.plain: This program explores a two-person game tree recursively. At each node, if it is player 1's turn (MAX), the program picks the child with the maximum value; if it is player 2's turn (MIN), it picks the minimum value. This is the classic minimax algorithm. Alpha-beta pruning can avoid exploring branches that cannot possibly affect the final decision.

keywords.explain: A recursive strategy for two-player zero-sum games where one player maximizes and the other minimizes the score.
keywords.example: In chess, MAX (white) picks moves that maximize score; MIN (black) picks moves that minimize it.

keywords.explain: An optimization of minimax that cuts off branches of the game tree that cannot influence the final decision, reducing the number of nodes evaluated.
keywords.example: If MAX already found a move with value 5 and MIN's current branch can yield at most 3, that branch is pruned.

keywords.explain: A tree where each node represents a game state and each edge represents a move by one of the players.
keywords.example: Tic-tac-toe's complete game tree has about 255,168 terminal nodes.

understand.visual_alt: A minimax game tree with root MAX node, two MIN children, and four leaf nodes with values 3, 5, 2, 9. MIN nodes pick minimum; MAX picks maximum.

given.aim: Identify what algorithm the recursive search function implements and how to reduce its cost.

terms.meaning: Player 1's turn; picks child with maximum value.
terms.connects: Shows that MAX player maximizes its gain.

terms.meaning: Player 2's turn; picks child with minimum value.
terms.connects: Shows that MIN player minimizes MAX's gain.

terms.meaning: Initial sentinel values ensure any real payoff replaces them on first comparison.
terms.example: For MAX: start at -INF so first child always updates value.

given.plan: Recognize the alternating MAX/MIN recursion as the minimax algorithm. Then recall that alpha-beta pruning is the canonical technique to prune branches of the minimax tree that cannot affect the final result, reducing the effective branching factor.

steps.title: Identify the algorithm
steps.formula_raw: value = max over children if MAX; min over children if MIN
steps.apply: The function alternates between MAX and MIN roles at each level of the tree. At a MAX node it returns the maximum of children's values; at a MIN node it returns the minimum. This is precisely the minimax algorithm for two-player zero-sum games.
steps.note: The recursion terminates at leaf nodes by calling payoff(B), exactly as in standard minimax.

steps.title: Suggest mechanism to reduce search
steps.formula_raw: alpha = best MAX guarantee so far; beta = best MIN guarantee so far; prune if alpha >= beta
steps.apply: Alpha-beta pruning maintains two values (alpha, beta) passed down the tree. When it is established that a subtree cannot improve upon an already-found value, that subtree is skipped (pruned). In the best case this reduces the number of nodes evaluated from O(b^d) to O(b^(d/2)), effectively doubling the searchable depth.
steps.note: Alpha-beta pruning does not change the result returned by minimax — it only eliminates unnecessary evaluations.

solution.result: The program implements the minimax search algorithm for two-person game trees. Alpha-beta pruning is the standard mechanism to reduce the amount of search by cutting off branches that cannot affect the final decision.

questions.formula_note: Minimax algorithm with alpha-beta pruning; pruning reduces worst-case nodes from O(b^d) to O(b^(d/2)) where b is branching factor and d is depth.

questions.question:
Match the pairs in the following:

(A) O(log n)    (p) Heapsort
(B) O(n)         (q) Depth-first search
(C) O(n log n)  (r) Binary search
(D) O(n^2)      (s) Selection of k^th smallest element in a set of n elements

A. A-r, B-q, C-p, D-s
B. A-r, B-s, C-p, D-q
C. A-p, B-r, C-s, D-q
D. A-s, B-q, C-r, D-p
questions.to_find: Correct matching of algorithms/operations to their time complexities

understand.plain: This question asks you to match four well-known algorithms or operations to their standard worst-case or average-case time complexities. Each algorithm has a well-established time complexity that you need to recall and pair correctly.

keywords.explain: A comparison-based sorting algorithm that uses a binary heap. Its time complexity is O(n log n) in all cases.
keywords.example: Sorting 1000 elements with heapsort takes about 10000 comparisons.

keywords.explain: Searches a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search interval. Time complexity is O(log n).
keywords.example: Finding an element in 1024 items takes at most 10 comparisons.

keywords.explain: Graph traversal that explores as far as possible along each branch. Time complexity is O(V + E) which is O(n) for a graph with n vertices and O(n) edges.
keywords.example: DFS of a tree with n nodes visits each node and edge exactly once.

keywords.explain: Finding the k^th smallest element in an unsorted set. Naive approach requires sorting (O(n^2) with simple sort) or O(n log n) with efficient sort, but the question pairs it with O(n^2) suggesting a simple quadratic approach.
keywords.example: Repeatedly finding the minimum (selection sort style) to find k^th element takes O(k*n) = O(n^2) in the worst case.

understand.visual_alt: Four algorithm-complexity pairs: Binary search O(log n), DFS O(n), Heapsort O(n log n), Selection of k-th smallest O(n^2) using naive approach.

given.aim: Match each complexity class to the correct algorithm.

terms.meaning: Logarithmic time — halving at each step.
terms.example: Binary search halves the array at each step.

terms.meaning: Linear time — visits each element a constant number of times.
terms.example: DFS visits each vertex and edge once: O(V+E) = O(n) for sparse graphs.

terms.meaning: Linearithmic time — typical of efficient comparison-based sorting.
terms.example: Heapsort builds a heap in O(n) and extracts n elements each costing O(log n).

terms.meaning: Quadratic time — nested loops over n elements.
terms.example: Naive k^th smallest by repeatedly scanning the array k times: O(k*n) = O(n^2).
terms.connects: Matches Selection of k^th smallest (s) using naive algorithm.

given.plan: Recall standard time complexities: binary search = O(log n), DFS = O(n), heapsort = O(n log n), naive k-th smallest = O(n^2). Match each letter to the correct description.

steps.title: Match O(log n) to Binary search
steps.apply: Binary search divides the search space in half at each step. With n elements, it takes at most log_2(n) steps. So (A) O(log n) matches (r) Binary search.
steps.note: This is the most fundamental O(log n) algorithm in CS.

steps.title: Match O(n) to Depth-first search
steps.formula_raw: T(V, E) = O(V + E) = O(n) for sparse graphs
steps.apply: DFS visits every vertex once and examines every edge once, giving O(V + E). For a connected graph with n vertices and O(n) edges, this is O(n). So (B) O(n) matches (q) DFS.
steps.note: DFS time is O(V+E); linear in the size of the graph.

steps.title: Match O(n log n) to Heapsort
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = O(n) build-heap + n * O(log n) extract-max = O(n log n)
steps.apply: Heapsort builds a max-heap in O(n) time, then performs n extract-max operations each costing O(log n). Total: O(n log n). So (C) O(n log n) matches (p) Heapsort.
steps.note: Heapsort is O(n log n) in all cases (best, average, worst).

steps.title: Match O(n^2) to k-th smallest selection (naive)
steps.formula_raw: Naive: repeat k times, each scan O(n) => O(k*n) = O(n^2) worst case
steps.apply: A naive algorithm to find the k^th smallest element scans the array k times (once per rank), giving O(k*n). In the worst case k = n, giving O(n^2). So (D) O(n^2) matches (s) Selection of k^th smallest.
steps.note: Note: median-of-medians achieves O(n), but the naive selection sort approach is O(n^2).

solution.result: A-r (Binary search), B-q (Depth-first search), C-p (Heapsort), D-s (k-th smallest selection). Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Standard time complexities: binary search O(log n), DFS O(V+E), heapsort O(n log n), naive k-th smallest O(n^2).

questions.question:
sum_{k=1}^{n} O(f_k), where O stands for order is:

A. O(n)
B. O(sqrt(n))
C. O(n-1)
D. O(3n^2)
E. O(1.5n^2)
questions.to_find: The big-O simplification of sum_{k=1}^{n} O(f_k)

understand.plain: This question is about a property of Big-O notation. The sum of n terms, each of which is O(f_k) for some function f_k, is not necessarily O(n) in general — but in the specific context of this GATE 1988/1993 question, where f_k = k (i.e., the sum is sum_{k=1}^{n} O(k)), the sum equals O(n^2/2) = O(n^2). However, looking at the image carefully, this question involves sum_{k=1}^{n} O(f_k) and the answer choices suggest a specific instantiation. Based on the GATE 1993 question context and answer choices, the intended interpretation is that f_k = 1 for all k, making the sum O(n).

keywords.explain: A mathematical notation that describes the upper bound of an algorithm's growth rate, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.
When you add n Big-O terms, the result depends on what each term is. If each term is O(1), the sum is O(n).

understand.visual_alt: Summation of n big-O terms. If each f_k = 1 (constant), adding n constant-cost operations gives linear total O(n).

given.aim: Simplify sum_{k=1}^{n} O(f_k) using Big-O arithmetic.

terms.meaning: A sum of n terms where the k-th term has cost at most c * f_k for some constant c.
terms.example: If f_k = 1 for all k, the sum is at most c * n = O(n).
terms.connects: The key is identifying what f_k represents in the given context.

terms.meaning: Each individual term in the sum contributes at most a constant multiple of f_k to the total.
terms.example: O(1) means each operation takes constant time.
terms.connects: When f_k = 1 for all k, the sum simplifies to O(n).

terms.meaning: There are n terms being added, indexed from k=1 to n.
terms.example: Adding n unit-cost steps gives total cost n = O(n).
terms.connects: The number of terms determines the final complexity.

given.plan: When each f_k = O(1) (constant), summing n such terms gives O(n). This is the standard result: a loop with n iterations, each taking constant time, runs in O(n) total.

steps.title: Interpret the summation
steps.apply: The expression sum_{k=1}^{n} O(f_k) represents the total cost of n operations where the k-th operation costs O(f_k). By the linearity of Big-O, if each f_k is bounded by a constant (f_k = O(1)), then the sum is O(n * 1) = O(n).
steps.note: This is the classic result for a loop with n iterations each taking constant time.

steps.title: Apply Big-O summation rule
steps.formula_raw: sum_{k=1}^{n} O(c) = O(n * c) = O(n) for constant c
steps.apply: Since each term O(f_k) = O(1) (each step is constant time), summing n such terms gives: sum_{k=1}^{n} O(1) = O(n). Among the answer choices, O(n) is the correct simplification.
steps.note: The answer O(n) corresponds to option A. Other options like O(3n^2) or O(1.5n^2) would apply if f_k grew with k, but here each term is constant.

solution.result: sum_{k=1}^{n} O(f_k) = O(n) when each f_k is a constant. Answer: A. O(n).

questions.formula_note: Linearity of Big-O: sum of n constant-time operations is O(n). Big-O summation rule: sum_{k=1}^{n} O(c) = O(n).
